Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Unleashing the power of CRISPR-Cas mediated gene editing of ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453

SUMMARY:
Thermophiles genome editing _via_ CRISPR-Cas to enhance the biofuel production The latest discovery of the CRISPR-Cas system should provide a new channel in the creation of microbial biorefineries through site- specific gene editing that might boost the generation of biofuels from extremophiles. Particular attention is being paid to the use of CRISPR-Cas in thermophilic microorganisms and the ways in which genetic editing might improve the organisms' capacity for the production of biofuels. It underlines the promise of tools for genetic engineering, in particular CRISPR-Cas, to enhance several elements of biofuel production, such as the efficiency of hydrolysis, the inhibition of metabolic pathway, and substrate utilisation. In this regard, advanced molecular approaches, including marker-assisted breeding, multi-omics approaches, and gene-editing technologies, can be employed to genetically engineer these crops for enhanced biofuel production efficiency.

FULL CONTENT:
Revolutionizing biofuel generation: Unleashing the power of CRISPR-Cas mediated gene editing of extremophiles - ScienceDirect

Revolutionizing biofuel generation: Unleashing the power of CRISPR-Cas mediated gene editing of extremophiles
=============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Diksha Garg a, Mahesh Kumar Samota b, Nicholas Kontis c d, Niketan Patel c d, Saroj Bala a, Alexandre Soares Rosado c d

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micres.2023.127443](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micres.2023.127443 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0944501323001453&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

[Molecular biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/molecular-biology "Learn more about Molecular biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques like gene editing have altered the specific genes in micro-organisms to increase their efficiency to produce biofuels. This review paper investigates the outcomes of [Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/crispr "Learn more about Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CRISPR) for gene editing in extremophilic micro-organisms to produce biofuel. Commercial production of biofuel from [lignocellulosic waste](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/lignocellulosic-wastes "Learn more about lignocellulosic waste from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is limited due to various constraints. A potential strategy to enhance the capability of [extremophiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/extremophile "Learn more about extremophiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to produce biofuel is gene-editing _via_ CRISPR-Cas technology. The efficiency of [intracellular enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cell-enzyme "Learn more about intracellular enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") like [cellulase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellulase "Learn more about cellulase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hemicellulose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/hemicellulose "Learn more about hemicellulose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in extremophilic bacteria, fungi and [microalgae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microalga "Learn more about microalgae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been increased by alteration of genes associated with [enzymatic activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-activity "Learn more about enzymatic activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [thermotolerance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/critical-thermal-maximum "Learn more about thermotolerance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). extremophilic microbes like [Thermococcus kodakarensis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/thermococcus-kodakarensis "Learn more about Thermococcus kodakarensis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Thermotoga maritima](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/thermotoga-maritima "Learn more about Thermotoga maritima from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Thermus thermophilus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/thermus-thermophilus "Learn more about Thermus thermophilus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Pyrococcus furiosus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pyrococcus-furiosus "Learn more about Pyrococcus furiosus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [Sulfolobus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/sulfolobus "Learn more about Sulfolobus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sp. are explored for biofuel production. The conversion of lignocellulosic biomass into biofuels involves [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hydrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzymatic-hydrolysis "Learn more about hydrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and fermentation. The challenges like off-target effect associated with use of [extremophiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/extremophile "Learn more about extremophiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for biofuel production is also addressed. The appropriate regulations are required to maximize effectiveness while minimizing off-target cleavage, as well as the total biosafety of this technique. The latest discovery of the CRISPR-Cas system should provide a new channel in the creation of microbial biorefineries through site- specific gene editing that might boost the generation of biofuels from [extremophiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/extremophile "Learn more about extremophiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Overall, this review study highlights the potential for genome editing methods to improve the potential of [extremophiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/extremophile "Learn more about extremophiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to produce biofuel, opening the door to more effective and environmentally friendly biofuel production methods.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001416)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001404)

Keywords

Biofuels

CRISPR-Cas

Genetic regulation

Off-target effect

Fermentation

1. Introduction
---------------

The emergence of petroleum-based fuel had an amazing impact on industrial growth, infrastructural development, power generation and several other such concepts. [Fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about Fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") now provide over eighty percent of the world's energy. The high usage and demand of fossil fuels however is concomitant with an ever increasing environmental and economic global concern for its sustainable production and usage. In addition to this, fossil fuels have several detrimental impacts on earth, its complex ecosystems, [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and on humans. They generate more than 75% of global greenhouse gases and approximately 80% of [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") emissions exacerbating global warming. Even when the problems and disadvantages are clear and well understood, numerous reports show that the production of oil, gas and coal will practically double by 2030. By substituting conventional fossil fuels with biofuels that may be made from renewable organic material, many of these disadvantages can be reduced. It is important to promote [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") like biofuels, since they offer a more economical and environmentally friendly alternative ([Darda et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib24)). This alternative will also be beneficial in future logistics, and warehouse industries, requiring minimal mechanical changes ([Singhal et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib82)). Many microbial strains are used for biofuel production by fermentation such as [Saccharomyces cerevisiae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/saccharomyces-cerevisiae "Learn more about Saccharomyces cerevisiae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_._ This fungus is widely used for [ethanol production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ethanol-production "Learn more about ethanol production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_via_ fermentation of [monosaccharides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/monosaccharide "Learn more about monosaccharides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on a commercial level. Also, different bacteria such as [Clostridium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/clostridium "Learn more about Clostridium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_thermosaccharolyticum_, [Zymomonas mobilis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/zymomonas-mobilis "Learn more about Zymomonas mobilis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), _C. thermohydrosulfuricum_, [Thermoanaerobacter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/thermoanaerobacter "Learn more about Thermoanaerobacter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_brockii_, _T. mathranii_, and _T. ethanolicus_ are employed for fermentation. Biofuel production greatly depends on the microbial strains. Fortunately, these can be improved by site-specific genome editing ([Adegboye et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib3)). Current technologies, such as biochemical and molecular engineering, [bioengineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioengineering "Learn more about bioengineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and fermentation engineering, can play a vital role in improving biofuel production. Due to the simplicity and efficiency of fermentation, microorganisms are recognized as promising sources for biofuel synthesis _via_ numerous metabolic processes, including [glucose catabolism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/glycolysis "Learn more about glucose catabolism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), lipids [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/alpha-oxidation "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the [isoprenoid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/isoprenoid "Learn more about isoprenoid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pathway ([Joshi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib42)). Despite this, the fermentation of microorganisms can generate certain by-products that can lead to the impairment of the desired products. This issue is quite prevalent in large-scale manufacturing systems, which in turn has called for a drastic change in their current strategy of efficient and cost-effective biofuel production. With the continuous advance of [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and genome engineering, it is now possible to implement various microbial strains to utilize the mechanism of [hydrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzymatic-hydrolysis "Learn more about hydrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") which can convert complicated substrates into simple fermentable forms ([Celińska et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib19)).

The widely used and studied CRISPR-Cas system can be used to mediate selective genetic manipulation in industrial microbial strains such as the aforementioned to achieve optimal biofuel production. CRISPR-Cas technology, has revolutionized the use of gene editing tools. Biorefineries is one of the many fields, CRISPR-Cas has influenced. This tool is both easy and precise and could enable modification of different components in biofuel production, such as better hydrolysis, enhanced suppressing, and the targeting of competing metabolic routes. Compatibility with solvents and substrate utilization are two factors that will have a significant impact on future commercial successful applications.

The purpose of this review study is to investigate whether or not the editing of genomes using CRISPR-Cas technology might result in an increase in the amount of biofuel that is produced. Particular attention is being paid to the use of CRISPR-Cas in [thermophilic microorganisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/thermophilic-microorganisms "Learn more about thermophilic microorganisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the ways in which genetic editing might improve the organisms' capacity for the production of biofuels. The purpose of this study is to examine the impacts that genome editing has on metabolic pathways, as well as the attempts that have been made to limit off-target effects, and the overall influence that this has on the production of biofuels. In addition, this article illustrates the eco-friendliness of biofuels and the ways in which CRISPR-Cas technologies might help make the production of large-scale industrial biofuels less harmful to the environment.

The originality of this review study resides in the fact that the author chose to concentrate specifically on the use of CRISPR-Cas technology in [extremophiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/extremophile "Learn more about extremophiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the generation of biofuel. It underlines the promise of tools for genetic engineering, in particular CRISPR-Cas, to enhance several elements of biofuel production, such as the efficiency of hydrolysis, the inhibition of metabolic pathway, and substrate utilisation. This review also discusses the difficulties that come with manipulating genes in a way that produces off-target effects and investigates potential solutions to these problems. The effects of off-target genetic manipulation in metabolic pathways and efforts of reducing these will also be discussed. In this research, insights into the potential of CRISPR-Cas technology for the production of sustainable biofuels are provided by highlighting the environmentally friendly nature of biofuels and the role that CRISPR-Cas plays in enhancing the total output of biofuels.

2. Data collection and analysis
-------------------------------

One hundred thirty publications on biofuels, [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques, CRISPR-Cas, gene editing, and off-target impact were found in this review's Web of Science™ Core Collection. The non-commercial visualisation of similarities (VOS) viewer VOS viewer 1.6.18 ([https://www.vosviewer.com/](https://www.vosviewer.com/)) created the graphical mapping of the term co-occurrence and co-authorship. Future research orientations may be strongly predicted by the amount of published material. Different academic disciplines may be categorized using a keyword list. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#fig0005) displays maps of the co-citation of the most-cited terms from 130 publications published between 2018 and 2022 using network visualisation, density visualisation, and overlay visualisation techniques. The [bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/volume "Learn more about bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of recent study has been devoted to developing new biofuels, and it's been stated that one of the most extensively researched areas with potential uses is genetic manipulation by CRISPR-Cas.

3. Generations of biofuel sources
---------------------------------

### 3.1. 1st generation

First generation biofuels are produced by [corn](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/maize "Learn more about corn from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), wheat, soybeans and other such [feedstock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/feedstock "Learn more about feedstock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The sources can separated to starch and [sugar crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sugar-crops "Learn more about sugar crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [oil seeds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/seed-oils "Learn more about oil seeds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") respectively for the manufacture of [bioethanol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/bioethanol "Learn more about bioethanol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [biodiesel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/biodiesel "Learn more about biodiesel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#fig0010)). The carbohydrates obtained from these types of feedstock as well as in the sources of other generations, can undergo fermentation with specific [enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") produced by microbes that excel in [pentose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pentose "Learn more about pentose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sugar metabolism to [bioethanol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioethanol "Learn more about bioethanol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The production of [biodiesel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biodiesel "Learn more about biodiesel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is also sought out after, through the trans-esterification of straight vegetable oils. Their cheap production allows for under-developed countries to have a near inexhaustible form of fuel ([Alaswad et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib5)). The use of first generation biofuel sources come with numerous disadvantages, however. First and foremost, there is a significant competition with [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the available land needed for cultivation. This has caused a problem of food _vs_ biofuel to arise. Furthermore, to produce the biomass for biofuel production intense use of fertilizers and agrochemicals are used. This has led to the reduction in water and soil quantity further aggravating the problem of food availability. There are several other drawbacks of first generation sources. Greenhouse gas emissions, [deforestation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/deforestation "Learn more about deforestation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), biodiversity loss _etc._ further increase the environmental and social negative impact of food crops being used as biofuel. To lessen these effects, a less expensive and readily available biomass was needed. The answer lay in the waste produced from municipalities, agriculture and forests ([Ho et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944501323001453#bib37)).

# Advances in CRISPR/Cas9 technology: shaping the future of photosynthetic microorganisms for biofuel production

### Affiliations

# Advances in CRISPR/Cas9 technology: shaping the future of photosynthetic microorganisms for biofuel production

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Use of fossil fuels causes environmental issues due to its inefficiency and and imminent depletion. This has led to interest in identifying alternative and renewable energy sources such as biofuel generation from photosynthetic organisms. A wide variety of prokaryotic and eukaryotic microorganisms, known as microalgae, have the potential to be economical and ecologically sustainable in the manufacture of biofuels such as bio-hydrogen, biodiesel, bio-oils, and bio-syngas. By using contemporary bioengineering techniques, the innate potential of algae to produce biomass of superior quality may be enhanced. In algal biotechnology, directed genome modification via RNA-guided endonucleases is a new approach. CRISPR/Cas systems have recently been frequently used to modify the genetic makeup of several aquatic and freshwater microalgae. The majority of research has used the Cas9-driven Type II system, one of two classes and six unique kinds of CRISPR systems, to specifically target desired genes in algae, and knock them out and down, or both. Using CRISPR technology to modify its genetic makeup, microalgae has produced more biomass and increased in lipid content. This review highlights the attempts made so far to target microalgae genome modification, discusses the prospects for developing the CRISPR platform for large-scale genome modification of microalgae, and identifies the opportunities and challenges in the development and distribution of CRISPR/Cas9 components.

enriched_points.point_title: CRISPR-Cas Gene Editing for Enhanced Production
enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing CRISPR-Cas technology allows for precise genetic modifications in extremophiles, enhancing their biofuel production capabilities by improving metabolic pathways and substrate utilization.
Agricultural waste serves as a sustainable feedstock for biofuel production, reducing environmental impact while promoting circular economy practices in bioenergy conversion.
Advanced microbial processes enhance the conversion efficiency of biomass to biofuels, optimizing enzymatic activity and minimizing by-product formation for greater yield.
